title: Tokens, next-token modeling and context

canonicalExplanation.summary: A language model represents text as tokens and estimates a conditional distribution for the next token from finite context; decoding repeatedly samples or selects from those distributions.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A toy bigram table assigns different next-token probabilities after one context token and greedy versus sampled decoding produces different valid continuations. Your goal is to trace a toy token-generation example and identify unsupported capability claims. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Convolution, sequence and attention roles; Natural-language processing. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Bounded operational model
sections.body: A language model represents text as tokens and estimates a conditional distribution for the next token from finite context; decoding repeatedly samples or selects from those distributions. Any capability or quality claim remains limited to an explicit task, population, conditions, baseline, and recorded evidence.

sections.heading: Failure boundary and non-claim
sections.body: Tokenization is model-specific, context is finite, probabilities are not truth scores, training data is not a queryable citation database, decoding changes variability, and fluent continuation does not prove reasoning or understanding. The learner must reject this overclaim: An LLM retrieves the most relevant stored sentence and its next-token probability measures whether the resulting claim is true.

includes: Explain LLM generation as conditional token prediction with finite context rather than database retrieval or understanding proof.
Trace a toy token-generation example and identify unsupported capability claims.
excludes: Tokenization is model-specific, context is finite, probabilities are not truth scores, training data is not a queryable citation database, decoding changes variability, and fluent continuation does not prove reasoning or understanding.

outcomes: Trace a toy token-generation example and identify unsupported capability claims.

examples.title: Tokens, next-token modeling and context: bounded direct case
examples.situation: A toy bigram table assigns different next-token probabilities after one context token and greedy versus sampled decoding produces different valid continuations.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the exact data, state, metric, or tool behavior: A toy bigram table assigns different next-token probabilities after one context token and greedy versus sampled decoding produces different valid continuations.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Verify the claim boundary with: Interpret generation as conditional token prediction within finite context; verify factual claims against authorized sources and do not treat model probability as truth.
examples.result: This example supports only the atomic outcome because Interpret generation as conditional token prediction within finite context; verify factual claims against authorized sources and do not treat model probability as truth.

examples.title: Tokens, next-token modeling and context: stress and transfer case
examples.situation: The model confidently states an unsupported date because plausible token continuation is not evidence retrieval or source verification.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name the changed population, input, assumption, or failure: The model confidently states an unsupported date because plausible token continuation is not evidence retrieval or source verification.

Step 2 — Keep the non-claim visible: Tokenization is model-specific, context is finite, probabilities are not truth scores, training data is not a queryable citation database, decoding changes variability, and fluent continuation does not prove reasoning or understanding.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting overclaim: An LLM retrieves the most relevant stored sentence and its next-token probability measures whether the resulting claim is true.
